Lojong — training the mind in compassion

A transformative eight-week programme in the Tibetan Buddhist art of mind training. Learn to work with difficult emotions, cultivate genuine kindness, and develop the courage to open your heart to yourself and others.
Hands holding a small plant, symbol of nurturing

## About Lojong

Lojong — "mind training" — is a set of practices from the Tibetan Buddhist tradition that systematically train us to transform even the most difficult experiences into the path of compassion. These methods are non-sectarian and have been adopted by teachers and practitioners across traditions worldwide.

## What the Course Covers

The programme follows the classical Seven Points of Mind Training, translated into a contemporary setting:

1. **The foundation** — how the mind actually works
2. **Absolute bodhichitta** — resting in openness
3. **Relative bodhichitta** — tonglen (sending and taking)
4. **Transforming adversity** — working with difficulty
5. **Lifetime practices** — integrating compassion into daily life
6. **Commitments** — what to adopt and abandon
7. **Guidelines for daily life** — practical instructions

## Research and Benefits

Compassion training has been shown in clinical studies to reduce stress, increase resilience, and improve relationships. Participants regularly report feeling more at ease with themselves and others, and more able to respond rather than react in difficult situations.

A six-week exploration of how meditation practice can transform the way we live, work, and relate. Practical, grounded, and immediately applicable to the challenges of modern life.
Morning light through a window onto a wooden table with tea

## What We Explore

This course looks at how meditation is not just something we do on a cushion — it is a way of being in the world. We explore how to bring awareness and compassion into our work, our relationships, and even the most mundane daily activities.

Topics include:

- Working with stress and the busy mind
- Mindful communication
- Meditation and sleep
- Technology and the distracted mind
- Finding meaning and purpose

## Format

Each Wednesday evening we begin with a guided meditation, then explore a theme through teaching, small group discussion, and reflection practices. You will also receive simple home practices to explore during the week.

This course pairs well with Introduction to Meditation, though it stands completely on its own.

A free weekly drop-in meditation session open to everyone. Thirty minutes of guided meditation followed by a short teaching and informal tea. No booking required — just come.
Candles and cushions in a softly lit meditation room

## Open to Everyone

Our Thursday evening sitting is the heart of our community week. It is completely free, requires no booking, and welcomes everyone — whether you have been meditating for decades or are curious for the first time.

## Format

- **6:00pm** — Doors open, quiet settling time
- **6:15pm** — Guided meditation (30 minutes)
- **6:45pm** — Short teaching or reading (15 minutes)
- **7:00pm** — Informal tea and conversation

## What to Expect

We keep things simple and unhurried. There is no expectation to speak, participate, or share. Come as you are.

A donation bowl is by the door. All donations support our scholarship fund, which helps those who cannot afford our courses attend free of charge.

Learn to settle your mind and find genuine peace. This friendly, step-by-step course introduces the core techniques of Buddhist meditation in a completely accessible way — no experience, no belief required.
Person sitting in meditation in a sunlit room

## What You Will Learn

Over eight evenings you will discover that meditation is simpler than you think, and more transformative than you might expect. We begin with the basics — posture, breath, and how to relate to the wandering mind — and build steadily through more subtle techniques.

Each class includes:
- A short teaching
- Guided meditation (30 minutes)
- Discussion and Q&A

## Who This Course Is For

This course is for complete beginners and for people who have tried to meditate but found it hard to get started on their own. You do not need any background in Buddhism or any particular belief. The techniques are universal and have been practised for centuries.

## What to Bring

Just yourself. Cushions and chairs are provided. We ask that you come with a sincere wish to learn and an open mind.

## Registration

Places are limited to 16 participants per course to ensure personal attention. Early registration is encouraged.

A half-day sitting session — four hours of guided and silent meditation with breaks, tea, and a short teaching. Open to anyone with basic meditation experience.

## A Day of Deepening

Half-day sessions offer something different from a weekly class: the chance to settle in, let the busyness of the week dissolve, and spend a sustained period in practice.

We will work with breath meditation and open awareness, moving through the arc of a full sitting day in miniature.

Suitable for those who have some experience of meditation, either from our courses or elsewhere.

## Format

- 9:00am — Arrival and settling
- 9:15am — Guided meditation
- 10:30am — Tea break
- 10:45am — Silent sitting
- 11:30am — Teaching and discussion
- 1:00pm — Close

A simple lunch is available afterwards for those who wish to stay and connect.

A three-day residential retreat at our centre exploring the practices of stillness, rest, and natural awareness. Suitable for those with at least some prior meditation experience.

## About the Retreat

The spring season invites renewal. This three-day residential retreat creates a container of silence, practice, and gentle inquiry — a rare opportunity to step back from the pace of daily life and reconnect with what matters most.

Led by Sarah Morrison and David Chen, the retreat draws on shamatha (calm abiding), the practice of rest, and teachings on natural awareness from both the Tibetan and Zen traditions.

## Schedule Overview

Each day follows a rhythm of sitting practice, gentle movement, teaching, and free time in nature. Meals are taken in silence. Evenings include optional group discussion.

- Saturday arrival from 4pm
- Practice begins Saturday evening
- Sunday full day of practice
- Monday practice and closing by 3pm

## What is Included

Room and board (shared accommodation), all meals (vegetarian), teaching, and practice sessions.

Bring comfortable clothing, a journal, and an open heart.
